key_points: Darnall is the worst dorm—small rooms, rats, and unwanted guests
Harbin had sewage issues from students flushing aluminum cans
Housing after freshman year is reportedly much better
key_quotes: I lived in that dorm in 1994. My daughter spent last summer there and I can say it has not changed one bit. Not one bit. The chair in her room was literally the same.
It definitely has small rooms, if not the smallest, but the biggest issue is the sporadic rat cases and some unwanted guests into the dorm hall.
last year harbin had sewage issues bc ppl were flushing aluminum cans

key_points: House parties are nearly nonexistent due to SNAP enforcement and neighbor complaints
Transferring in as a junior makes friend-making extremely difficult
Competitive club application process can be isolating and harmful to self-esteem
key_quotes: I genuinely had no idea how hard it would be at this school to make friends. Tried applying to clubs and got rejected from all of them.
Why is no one as fed up about what the social scene's unfulfilled potential as I am? House parties are almost non-existent.
The selection process is unnecessarily harmful to new students' self-esteem IMO, and the culture skews toxic.

key_points: Dining hall food draws typical complaints but is considered average to decent
Epicurean in Darnall now offers meal exchange, seen as a small win
Off-campus DC eating is expensive and strains budgets
key_quotes: Complaining about dining hall food is a ubiquitous college experience across every college not-named Bowdoin, but honestly the dining hall options at Georgetown are fine.
It's DC so eating out and getting treats will hurt the wallet.
At least epicurean is meal exchange now though, and that's downstairs!

key_points: Hustle and grind culture creates real stress and isolation
Transferring students report severe loneliness with little institutional support
Intense club competition and rejection culture damages student wellbeing
key_quotes: I've been in your shoes also. It is very isolating, and I transferred out of GU and I never regretted it.
Georgetown acts like going there sets you up for life and it just doesn't. I also could not get internships in college.
The selection process is unnecessarily harmful to new students' self-esteem IMO.

key_points: Financial aid office is slow and disorganized—documents sat unreviewed for weeks
Some students receive no need-based aid despite $100k/year sticker price
Aid packages can be generous for some; wildly insufficient for others
key_quotes: I JUST got my rd financial aid award after calling the financial aid office multiple times and discovering they js let my documents sit there.
There is no way my family can afford $100k per year, and taking out that much money in loans for undergrad just doesn't make sense.
Georgetown has some incompetent staff. You have no reason to feel regret.

key_points: SFS is world-class for international relations—unmatched DC access and faculty
STEM programs are weak relative to peers; physics students warned away
Pre-med advising is strong with good hospital access and small class sizes
key_quotes: Georgetown is #1 for international relations especially School of Foreign Service. Just like MIT is #1 for engineering.
People telling me don't go there for STEM.
Pre med at Georgetown is honestly fantastic. There is strong advising and smaller classes so you can get to know professors well.

key_points: University communicates poorly during emergencies—weather alerts come dangerously late
Financial aid office unresponsive; students struggle to reach anyone by phone
Internal admin described as barely functional even by current students
key_quotes: Unfortunately, GU is not very functional, at the administrative level. They barely communicate within.
American and Howard have both announced campus closures, and GWU has communicated to their students too—and Georgetown has said NOTHING.
How did you get ahold of Georgetown?? I'm in the same situation except they STILL are not picking up my calls.

key_points: Campus is visually stunning—Healy Hall and Riggs Library are iconic
Georgetown neighborhood is expensive and the area limits loud social activity
DC location is the school's biggest asset for politics, policy, and networking
key_quotes: This is the most beautiful campus and my personal favorite. I love the history, the structure details. Absolutely beautiful!
Georgetown exists in Georgetown and faces town and gown issues with the local community. Loud house parties annoy the local community.
I love how accessible the city is, and the direct pathway/opportunities you can get at Georgetown to the government are absolutely unparalleled.

key_points: SFS and MSB open strong doors in consulting, IB, and government
Georgetown name alone does not guarantee internships or job offers
DC location provides unmatched access to Hill internships and think tanks
key_quotes: Georgetown acts like going there sets you up for life and it just doesn't. I also could not get internships in college.
Georgetown has consulting clubs that lets you work with real companies. Insanely competitive to get into, but helps your resume.
Management consultancies usually like GT grads with solid academics right out of college.

key_points: At $65k+ tuition annually, cost-benefit ratio is questioned widely
Students without aid face potentially crushing debt for a non-STEM degree
For IR/policy careers, Georgetown's network may justify the premium
key_quotes: In 2024, annual tuition is 65K. Are they crazy? These students will spend the rest of their lives trying to pay off this four year debt.
Georgetown for $50K/year or Washington and Lee (Full-Ride)?
Attend UVA. Attend UVA. Attend UVA. The outcome will be probably identical, but the stress from the debt will encumber you for YEARS.

key_points: Pre-professional, politically ambitious, and hustle-oriented culture dominates
Socioeconomically skewed toward wealthy prep-school students—visible in daily life
Students who thrive are self-driven; those who need community support may struggle
key_quotes: It was always more pre-professional than other schools. One of my roommates came back from a weekend at Yale and told us 'I couldn't believe how different it was.'
There are rich snobs but there are also more people on scholarships than I expected. Main way socioeconomic differences manifest is the little things.
I am not a white, rich, private school student. I came for an education and want to hear every point of view.

key_points: **Administration:** Financial aid office loses documents and ignores student calls
**Social inclusion:** Conservative or non-conforming students report bullying by peers and professors
**Infrastructure:** Darnall dorm has active rat infestations and has not been renovated in decades
key_quotes: If you hold even one conservative view prepare to be bullied by other students and professors.
I lived in that dorm in 1994. My daughter spent last summer there and I can say it has not changed one bit. Not one bit.
Georgetown has some incompetent staff. You have no reason to feel regret.

key_points: Riggs Library inside Healy Hall is one of the most beautiful rooms in US academia
Wisemiller's Deli on 36th Street is a beloved local secret for students
Non-application clubs like The Voice and Radio are welcoming social alternatives
key_quotes: For pics, Riggs Library, which is inside Healey Hall, south end of the building, top floor.
Wisemillers Deli on 36th street—chicken madness, burger madness, peggy's special.
I highly recommend clubs that you don't need to apply for, like the Voice or the Radio.
